title: Ponte Dellן¿½ Angelo, Venice
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stop rowing! This one of our bye-canals
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er a certain bridge you have to cross
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s named, ן¿½Of the Angel:ן¿½ listen why!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The name ן¿½Of the Devilן¿½ too much appalls
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Venetian acquaintance, so, his the loss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the gain goes . . . look on high!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An angel visibly guards yon house:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Above each scutcheon, a pair, stands he,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Enfolds them with droop of either wing:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The familyן¿½s fortune were perilous
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did he thence depart, you will soon agree,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I hitch into verse the thing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, once on a time, this house belonged
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To a lawyer of note, with law and to spare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But also with overmuch lust of gain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the matter of law you were nowise wronged,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But alas for the lucre! He picked you bare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the bone. Did folk complain?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I exact,ן¿½ growled he, ן¿½workן¿½s rightful due:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis folk seek me, not I seek them.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Advice at its price! They succeed or fail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Get law in each case, and a lesson too:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Keep clear of the Courts, is advice ad rem:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ll remember, Iן¿½ll be bail!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, he pocketed fee without a qualm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What reason for squeamishness? Labor done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To play he betook him with lightened heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ate, drank, and made merry with song or Psalm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since the yoke of the Church is an easy one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fits neck nor causes smart.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brief: never was such an extortionate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rascal, the word has escaped my teeth!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet, (allן¿½s down in a book no ass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Indited, believe me!), this reprobate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was punctual at prayer-time: gold lurked beneath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alloy of the rankest brass.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, play the extortioner as he might,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fleece folk each day and all day long,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was this redeeming circumstance:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never lay down to sleep at night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he put up a prayer first, brief yet strong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Our Lady avert mischance!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now it happened at close of a fructuous week
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I must ask,ן¿½ quoth he, ן¿½some Saint to dine:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I want that widow well out of my ears
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With her ailing and wailing. Who bade her seek
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Redress at my hands? ן¿½She was wronged!ן¿½ Folk whine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If to Law wrong right appears.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Matteo da Bascio, heן¿½s my man!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No less than Chief of the Capucins:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His presence will surely suffumigate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My house, fools think lies under a ban
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If somebody loses what somebody wins.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hark, there he knocks at the grate!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Come in, thou blessed of Mother Church!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I go and prepare, to bid, that is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My trusty and diligent servitor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Get all things in readiness. Vain the search
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through Venice for one to compare with this.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My model of ministrants: for,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For, once again, nay, three times over.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My helpmateן¿½s an ape! so intelligent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I train him to drudge at household work:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He toils and he moils, I live in clover:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, you shall see! Thereן¿½s a goodly scent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From his cooking, or Iן¿½m a Turk!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Scarce need to descend and supervise:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll do it, however: wait here awhile!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, down to the kitchen gayly scuttles
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our host, nor notes the alarmed surmise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the holy man. ן¿½O depth of guile!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He blindly guzzles and guttles,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½While, who is it dresses the food and pour,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The liquor? Some fiend, I make no doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In likeness of, which of the loathly brutes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An ape! Where hides he? No bull that gores
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No bear that hugs, ן¿½t is the mock and flow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of an ape, fiendן¿½s face that suits.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So, out with thee, creature, wherever thou hidest!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I charge thee, by virtue of . . . right do I judge!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There skulks he perdue, crouching under the bed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well done! What, forsooth, in beastן¿½s shape thou confidest?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know and would name thee but that I begrudge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Breath spent on such carrion. Instead.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I adjure thee by--ן¿½ ן¿½Stay!ן¿½ laughed the portent that rose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From floor up to ceiling: ן¿½No need to adjure!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See Satan in person, late ape by command
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Him thou adjurest in vain. A saintן¿½s nose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scents brimstone though incense be burned for a lure.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet, hence! for Iן¿½m safe, understand!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis my charge to convey to fit punishmentן¿½s place
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This lawyer, my liegeman, for cruelty wrought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On his clients, the widow and orphan, poor souls
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He has plagued by exactions which proved lawן¿½s disgrace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made equity void and to nothingness brought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s pity. Fiends, on with fresh coals!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Stay!ן¿½ nowise confounded, withstands Hell its match:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How comes it, were truth in this story of thine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s punishment suffered a minuteן¿½s delay?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weeks, months have elapsed since thou squattedst at watch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a spring on thy victim: what caused thee decline
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Advantage till challenged to-day?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That challenge I meet with contempt,ן¿½ quoth the fiend.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thus much I acknowledge: the manן¿½s armed in mail:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wait till a jointן¿½s loose, then quick ply my claws.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy friendן¿½s one good custom, he knows not, has screened
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His flesh hitherto from what else would assail:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At ן¿½Save me, Madonna!ן¿½ I pause.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That prayer did the losel but once pretermit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My pounce were upon him. I keep me attent:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s in safety but till heן¿½s caught napping. Enough!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ay, enough!ן¿½ smiles the Saint, ן¿½for the biter is bit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The spy caught in somnolence. Vanish! Iן¿½m sent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To smooth up what fiends do in rough.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I Vanish? Through wall or through roof?ן¿½ the ripost
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grinned gayly. ן¿½My orders were, ן¿½Leave not unharmed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The abode of this lawyer! Do damage to prove
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½T was for something thou quittedst the land of the lost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To add to their number this unit!ן¿½ Though charmed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From descent there, on earth thatן¿½s above

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I may haply amerce him.ן¿½ ן¿½So do, and begone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I command thee! For, look! Though thereן¿½s doorway behind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And window before thee, go straight through the wall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave a breach in the brickwork, a gap in the stone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For who passes to stare at!ן¿½ ן¿½Spare speech! Iן¿½m resigned:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here goes!ן¿½ roared the goblin, as all.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wide bat-wings, spread arms and legs, tail out a-stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crash obstacles went, right and left, as he soared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or else sank, was clean gone through the hole anyhow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Saint returned thanks: then a satisfied gleam
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the bald polished pate showed that triumph was scored.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To dinner with appetite now!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down he trips. ן¿½In good time!ן¿½ smirks the host. ן¿½Didst thou scent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rich savor of roast meat? Where hides he, my ape?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look alive, be alert! Heן¿½s away to wash plates.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sit down, Saint! Whatן¿½s here? Dost examine a rent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the napkin thou twistest and twirlest?ן¿½ Agape . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ha, blood is it drips nor abates

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From thy wringing a cloth, late was lavendered fair?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What means such a marvel?ן¿½ ן¿½Just this does it mean:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I convince and convict thee of sin!ן¿½ answers straight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Saint, wringing on, wringing ever, oh, rare!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blood, blood from a napery snow not more clean.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A miracle shows thee thy state!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½See, blood thy extortions have wrung from the flesh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of thy clients who, sheep-like, arrived to be shorn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left thee, or fleeced to the quick or so flayed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That, behold, their blood gurgles and grumbles afresh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To accuse thee! Ay, down on thy knees, get up sworn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To restore! Restitution once made,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sin no more! Dost thou promise? Absolved, then, arise!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upstairs follow me! Art amazed at yon breach?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who battered and shattered and scattered, escape
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From thy purlieus obtaining? That Father of Lies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou wast wont to extol for his feats, all and each
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Devilן¿½s disguised as thine ape!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Be sure that our lawyer was torn by remorse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shed tears in a flood, vowed and swore so to alter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His ways that how else could our Saint but declare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was cleansed of past sin? ן¿½For sin future, fare worse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou undoubtedly wilt,ן¿½ warned the Saint, ן¿½shouldst thou falter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One whit!ן¿½ ן¿½Oh, for that have no care!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am firm in my purposed amendment. But, prithee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must ever affront and affright me yon gap?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who made it for exit may find it of use
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For entrance as easy. If, down in his smithy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He forges me fetters, when heated, mayhap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll up with an armful! Broke loose.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How bar him out henceforth?ן¿½ ן¿½Judiciously urged!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was the good manן¿½s reply. ן¿½How to balk him is plain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s nothing the Devil objects to so much,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So speedily flies from, as one of those purged
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of his presence, the angels who erst formed his train,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His, their emperor. Choose one of such!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Get fashioned his likeness and set him on high
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At back of the breach thus adroitly filled up:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Display him as guard of two scutcheons, thy arms:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I warrant no devil attempts to get by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And disturb thee so guarded. Eat, drink, dine, and sup,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In thy rectitude, safe from alarms!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So said and so done. See, the angel has place
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the Devil has passage! Allן¿½s down in a book.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gainsay me? Consult it! Still faithless? Trust me?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trust Father Boverio who gave me the case
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In his Annals, gets of it, by hook or by crook,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two confirmative witnesses: three

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are surely enough to establish an act:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thereby we learn, would we ascertain truth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To trust wise tradition which took, at the time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Note that served till slow history ventured on fact,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though folk have their fling at tradition forsooth!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Row, boys, fore and aft, rhyme and chime!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
